name: M.S. in Applied Statistics

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The 19 referenced courses form a coherent classical-statistics spine: foundations in MATH 662 Probability Distributions and MATH 665 Statistical Inference build to MATH 763 Generalized Linear Models and MATH 768 Probability Theory (deepestPrereqChain 3), with strong applied breadth in Regression (644), Design of Experiments (699), Sampling (698), Survival Analysis (659), and Clinical Trials (654) plus computing (660) and consulting (664). The menu also spans finance (604, 605), so it reads more as a broad well-ordered offering than a single tight track.
dimensions.suggestion: Group the elective menu into declared concentrations (e.g., biostatistics, industrial/DOE, financial) so students can see coherent pathways through the 19 courses.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph is healthy: missingCourseCount is 0 and deepestPrereqChain is 3, with explicit, sensible gates (MATH 665 requires 662; MATH 763 requires 662 and 665; MATH 768 sits at depth 3). The one cross-disciplinary dependency is MATH 604 Mathematical Finance, which requires FIN 641 and MATH 605.
dimensions.suggestion: Verify that MATH 604's finance prerequisite (FIN 641) is reachable for applied-statistics students, or label MATH 604 clearly as a financial-track elective.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes are present in the data, so the competencies a graduate should demonstrate are not stated on the page.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish outcomes such as designing experiments, building and validating regression/GLM models, and demonstrating statistical-computing proficiency.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: statedTotalCredits is 30 (a conventional M.S. total) and all substantive courses are 3 credits with a 0-credit seminar, implying roughly ten courses; however requirementGroups is 0 so the required-core vs. elective split is not visible and creditReconcileDelta is null.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish the required-core vs. elective credit split and a plan of study so the 30 credits are transparent.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Course descriptions are substantive and specific across the 19 courses and titles clearly signal content, but there is no plan-of-study grouping, no core/elective labeling, and no stated distribution, so structure must be inferred from prerequisites.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a plan-of-study table that labels core vs. elective courses and shows the intended term sequence.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The classical toolkit is strong (regression, GLM, DOE, sampling, survival analysis, clinical trials, SAS/R computing in MATH 660), but the program is notably light on modern data science: there is no statistical/machine-learning course, no Bayesian methods, and computing is SAS/R only with no Python.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a statistical/machine-learning course and a Bayesian-methods course, and introduce Python alongside SAS/R, to align with current industry demand.

descriptionQuality.justification: Descriptions are specific and informative rather than placeholder, articulating concrete content (e.g., MATH 665 on sufficiency, point estimation and testing; MATH 699 on randomization, block and factorial designs) across the 19 courses.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are stated as explicit course codes with sensible gates and no dangling references (missingCourseCount 0), e.g., MATH 665 requires 662 and MATH 763 requires 662 and 665; clarity is only mildly reduced by MATH 604's prerequisite string embedding course titles and MATH 630 carrying a long descriptive note.

missingTopics.rationale: Beyond MATH 763 Generalized Linear Models there is no course on modern predictive modeling (regularization, trees, cross-validation), a core expectation of a contemporary applied-statistics M.S.
No Bayesian or MCMC course appears among the 19 courses, a significant gap given the ubiquity of Bayesian methods in practice.
MATH 763 touches generalized estimating equations, but there is no dedicated longitudinal/mixed-models course for repeated-measures data.
MATH 660 covers SAS and R only; Python is now the dominant industry language for data analysis and should be represented.
Causal methods for observational data are increasingly central to applied statistics and are absent from the current course list.

emergingTrends.trend: Statistical machine learning and high-dimensional inference
emergingTrends.rationale: Natural extension of the MATH 665/763 inference-and-modeling core toward regularized and high-dimensional methods.
Tools like Stan and PyMC are now standard; the program has probability foundations (662, 768) to build on.
Version control, pipelines, and big-data tooling are expected professional skills that extend MATH 660's computing coverage.
Increasingly demanded in tech, health, and policy analytics, complementing the existing DOE course (699).

emergingTrends.trend: Modern experimental design (A/B and adaptive testing)
emergingTrends.rationale: Online experimentation and adaptive designs extend MATH 699 and MATH 654 toward current industry practice.

topSuggestions: Add a statistical/machine-learning course to close the biggest currency gap versus modern practice.
Add a Bayesian-methods course leveraging the existing probability foundations (MATH 662, 768).
Introduce Python alongside the SAS/R computing in MATH 660.
Declare concentrations and publish a plan of study with an explicit core-vs-elective credit split.
